coredata.dc:title: Solvent-free synthesis of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from glutathione for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercury(II) ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Weiping
Lu, Ya-Chun
Huang, Hong
Wang, Ai-Jun
Chen, Jian-Rong
Feng, Jiu-Ju
coredata.dc:description: In this report, a simple, low-cost, green, and solvent-free method was developed to prepare sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon nanoparticles (SNCNs) by one-pot solid-phase thermal treatment of glutathione, with the nitrogen and sulfur contents of 16.1% and 2.0%, respectively. SNCNs show bright blue photoluminescence with a high quantum yield of 39.9%, which is significantly quenched by trace Hg2+ via the Hg2+-thiol interactions and extended for label-free fluorescent detection of Hg2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity, even with high concentrations of interference (i.e., other metal ions). The fluorescence intensity increases linearly with Hg2+ from 1.0nM to 50.0μM, with the detection limit of 0.05nM (S/N =3).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solvent-free method
Fluorescence
Carbon nanoparticles
Glutathione
Mercury

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process for SNCNs and their application for Hg2+ detection.

(A) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra of SNCNs. Inset shows the photographs taken under visible light (left) and UV light of 365nm (right). (B) Emission spectra of SNCNs obtained by increasing the excitation wavelength from 371 to 450nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in text near the reference citation, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Typical TEM image (A) and size distribution (B) of SNCNs. Inset shows higher magnification TEM image of an individual nanoparticle.
XRD (A) and FT-IR (B) spectra of SNCNs.
Survey (A), and high-resolution C1s (B), N1s (C), and S2p (D) XPS spectra of SNCNs.
The fluorescence intensity at 442nm (excitation at 371nm) of SNCNs solutions at different pH (A) and storage time (B).

(A) Fluorescence responses of SNCNs in the presence of different metal ions (λ ex =371nm; [Mn+]=50.0μM). (B) The corresponding fluorescence intensities before (red) and after (green) the treatment with Hg2+ (50.0μM). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)

(A) The fluorescence spectra of SNCNs (20.0μg/mL) by treating with different Hg2+ concentrations. (B) The relationship between the F/F 0 and the Hg2+ concentrations. Inset shows the fluorescence quenching photographs of SNCNs with different Hg2+ concentrations (from left to right: 0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, and 50.0μM). The error bars represent variations among three separate measurements. (For interpretation of the references to color in text near the reference citation, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Solvent-free synthesis of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from glutathione for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercury(II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science, College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We reported a simple, direct, green and solvent-free method for preparation of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (SNCNs) by one-pot solid-phase thermal treatment of glutathione. The fluorescence can be quenched significantly by trace Hg2+ via the high-affinity of Hg2+ with thiol group of SNCNs. The as-prepared SNCNs are used as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for lable-free detection of Hg2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity.

In this report, a simple, low-cost, green, and solvent-free method was developed to prepare sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon nanoparticles (SNCNs) by one-pot solid-phase thermal treatment of glutathione, with the nitrogen and sulfur contents of 16.1% and 2.0%, respectively. SNCNs show bright blue photoluminescence with a high quantum yield of 39.9%, which is significantly quenched by trace Hg2+ via the Hg2+-thiol interactions and extended for label-free fluorescent detection of Hg2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity, even with high concentrations of interference (i.e., other metal ions). The fluorescence intensity increases linearly with Hg2+ from 1.0nM to 50.0μM, with the detection limit of 0.05nM (S/N =3).

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is one of the most prevalent toxic metals in the environment, which can enrich in the form of food chain and absorbed by humans, because it can easily pass through biological membranes (e.g., skin, respiratory, and gastrointestinal tissues), and even destroy the human body [1–3]. Thus, it is very important to develop an effective method for sensitive detection of trace amount of Hg2+.

Conventionally, atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy [4], auger-electron spectroscopy [5], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [6], and polarography [7] are usually employed for detection of heavy metal ions, owing to their efficiency and sensitivity. However, the above methods usually require complicated instrumentation or time-consuming sample treatment, which severely limit their practical and commercial applications [8].

Alternatively, nanoparticle-based fluorescent assay of Hg2+ has been developed, because of its high sensitivity, fast analysis, and non-sample-destructing. Generally, Au nanoparticles [9], Ag nanoparticles [10,11], carbon nanoparticles (CNs) [2,12], and CdSe nanodots [13] are widely used as fluorescence probes.

CNs are particularly popular for their high water-solubility, size-tunable photoluminescence, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and chemical stability [14–20], since photoluminescent CNs were discovered accidently ten years before [21]. Besides, CNs are different from traditional organic dyes [22] and semiconductor quantum dots [13], and thereby have broad applications in bio-imaging [23], optoelectronic devices [24], sensors [25–28], drug/gene delivery [29], and photocatalysis [30,31]. As a result, many methods have been developed, such as microwave-assisted [32–34] and hydrothermal method [35,36], ultrasonication [37], carbonization [38,39], surface passivation [40] and functionalization [41,42]. Among them, thermal treatment synthesis is regarded as a simple, direct, and efficient route for synthesis of CNs with novel properties [43,44].

CNs doped by hereroatoms have tunable properties, containing electronic characteristics, suface and local chemical features [45], as well as wide applications in bioimaging and (bio)sensors [46,47]. Now, extensive efforts have been made for preparation of nitrogen-doped CNs (N-CNs) from cocoon silk [48] and dimethylformamide (DMF) [49] for bioimaging, and from the mixture of glucose and glycine for detection of Fe3+ [50]. Besides, sulfur-incorporated CNs (S-CNs) were synthesized using the mixture of bis(2-ethylhexyl) sulfo-succinate sodium salt, decane, glucose, thioglycolic acid, and hexadecylamine, with the enhanced absorbance and photoluminescence intensity at longer wavelength (∼500nm) [51].

Lately, sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped CNs (SNCNs) have been prepared from hair fiber [52], the mixture of pyrrole and H2SO4 [53], and citric acid with thiourea [54], which provided a viable route to extend their applications in photocatalysis, bioimaging, biosensor, electronic devices, energy conversion and storage. For example, Dong et al. [55] prepared N,S-codoped CNs from a hybrid containing l -cysteine and citric acid, which provide deep insights to their photoluminescence (PL) enhancement mechanism. In another example, Guo et al. [56] synthesized N,S-codoped graphene through sulfate-reducing bacteria treating graphene oxide. Compared to bare CNs, CNs doped by heteroatoms remain almost all the advantages of blank CNs, and aviod their intrinsic weakness. For instance, pure CNs is usually self-quenched, which may limit their applications in bioanalysis [57], and the relatively low PL quantum yield [55]. This is ascribed to intramolecular ground-state dimer complex or energy transfer between adjacent CNs. Although several successful examples have been reported, it is still urgent to develop a simple, cost–effective, and environmentally benign approach for synthesis of NSCNs with novel properties.

Herein, fluorescent SNCNs were prepared by one-pot solid-phase thermal treatment of glutathione, without any further chemical modification (Fig. 1 ). The as-prepared SNCNs were employed as an effective fluorescent probe for trace detection of Hg2+ in water samples with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Glutathione, Hg(NO3)2, NaNO3, ZnSO4·7H2O, AgNO3, Mn(CH3COO)2·4H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, MgSO4, Ba(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, CdCl2·2.5H2O, FeCl3·2H2O, KCl, FeSO4·4H2O and Pb(NO3)2 were purchased from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the other chemicals were analytical grade and used as received. The aqueous solutions were prepared with twice-distilled water in the whole experiments.

For typical preparation of SNCNs by solid-phase thermal treatment of glutathione, 0.1g of glutathione was heated in a stainless steel autoclave at 260°C for 1h. The product was dissolved with 50mL water, centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 20min, and further purified with a 0.22μm filter membrane to discard non-fluorescent deposit. The supernatant was collected for further characterization.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded on a Lambda 950 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin–Elmer, USA). Fluorescence emission spectroscopy was carried out on a LS-45 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin–Elmer, UK). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were conducted on an Edinburgh FLS 920 photocounting system. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on a Philips PW3040/60 automatic powder diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was preformed on a Nicolet 670 FT-IR spectrometer in the form of KBr pellets. The fluorescence images were acquired on a Leica laser confocal fluorescence microscope (TCS SP5).

For the typical assay, 30μL of SNCNs suspension (2mg/mL) was put into 3mL of water, followed by the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+. The mixed solutions were remained static and reacted for 20min at room temperature, followed by recording the fluorescence quenching spectra with the excitation wavelength of 371nm.

The selectivity of SNCNs was examined by the interfered metal ions such as Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, K+, Cd2+ and Fe2+ under the identical conditions. The concentrations of Hg2+ and other metal ions were all 50.0μM. Meanwhile, for studying the interference, the SNCNs suspension was mixed with Hg2+ in the absence or presence of the interferent. The concentration of the interferent was the same as that of Hg2+ (50.0μM). The corresponding fluorescence spectra were quickly recorded after incubation of 20min.

As shown in Fig. 1, SNCNs were prepared by one-pot solid-phase thermal treatment of glutathione at 260°C for 1h. The formation of SNCNs mainly includes four stages: dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and surface passivation. Firstly, glutathione molecules are aromatized by intermolecular dehydration with a polymerization process. Then, the intermediates are carbonized to form carbon nuclei, followed by the subsequent formation of functionalized SNCNs [58,59].

Fig. 2 shows the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of SNCNs. There is a small peak emerged at 315nm in the UV–vis spectrum of SNCNs. It indicates the presence of carbonyl or conjugated carbonyl groups. Meanwhile, the maximum fluorescence peak is observed at 442nm when SNCNs are excited by the UV light of 371nm (Fig. 2A). As illustrated by the insets in Fig. 2A, the solution color of SNCNs is yellow under visible light (left), while a blue emission is observed upon exciting with UV light of 365nm (right).

Fig. 2B demonstrates that the fluorescence emission of SNCNs is excitation-dependent. Specifically, with the increase of the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence peak is red shifted and the peak intensity decreases (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). This is ascribed to the distribution of different emissive trap sites and the difference in particle size of SNCNs [46]. The excitation-dependent emission trend is consistent with those in the literature [46,60–63]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence quantum yield is about 39.9%, using quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1mol/L H2SO4, λ ex =371nm) as a reference. This value is comparable to the previous work [48,62,64,65], but larger than those prepared from orange juice [66]. It is mainly ascribed to the synergistic effects between nitrogen atoms in amide groups and sulfur atoms in SNCNs [55], along with the amide groups obtained from amidation process between carboxyl and amine groups. The other reason is the existence of one or more amine groups. Therefore, the quantum yield of SNCNs is enhanced under the optimal reaction conditions [67].

Representative TEM image shows that SNCNs have spherical structures, with an average diameter of 2.93nm (Fig. 3A and B). Moreover, high-resolution TEM image displays the clear lattice fringes with an interfringe distance of 0.203nm (inset in Fig. 2A), which is assigned to the (102) crystal planes of graphitic (sp2) carbon [8]. This observation is similar to those fabricated from graphite powder [68], but different from those prepared from green tea [69].

As illustrated in Fig. 4A, there is a representative diffraction peak located at 25.4° in the XRD spectrum of SNCNs, suggesting that the interlayer spacing of the (002) peak is 0.35nm. This value is similar to that of graphite (0.34nm) [70], confirming that carbonizing glutathione can produce graphite structures [71].

Fig. 4B shows the FT-IR spectrum of SNCNs, in which a broad peak at around 3260cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups, revealing a large quantity of hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of SNCNs. These results demonstrate that the resulting SNCNs own good hydrophilicity [54]. Furthermore, the band at 2610cm−1 is attributed to the vibrational absorption of the SH groups, while the peak at 1690cm−1 is assigned to the CO stretching mode. The peak at 1410cm−1 is originated from the CN [52], and the band at 1230cm−1 comes from the CS stretching vibration mode [54].

XPS was employed to examine the surface states and compositions of SNCNs. As can be seen in Fig. 5A, the representative XPS peaks at 164.1, 286.1, 400.1, and 532.1eV correspond to the elements of S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. For the high-resolution C1s region (Fig. 5B), there are three peaks observed at 284.8, 286.0, and 288.0eV, which are attributed to the CC, CO, and CO groups, respectively. And two peaks are emerged at 399.5 and 400.5eV (Fig. 5C), which are attributed to the CNC and N(C)3 groups, respectively. Besides, the S2p pattern (Fig. 5D) can be divided into two component peaks at 163.5 and 164.5eV, which are close to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of the CS covalent bonds of the thiophene-S for their spin-orbit couplings [52], respectively. The O1s spectrum shows two peaks at 531.1 and 532.1eV, which are assigned to the CO and COH/COC groups, respectively (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). Additionally, the chemical compositions of SNCNs are C 58.7%, N 16.1%, S 2.0%, and O 23.1%, based on the XPS data, revealing higher nitrogen content and relatively lower sulfur content, compared with other SNCNs prepared from the mixture of silica, SBA-15, sucrose, and thiourea [72], along with graphene oxide with the assistance of ammonium thiocyanate [73].

In order to optimize the fluorescence performance of SNCNs, we have investigated the effects of the reaction temperature and time. The PL intensity reaches the maximum at the reaction temperature of 260°C (Fig. S3A, Supporting Information) using the reaction time of 1h (Fig. S3B, Supporting Information), respectively. Therefore, 260°C and 1h were chosen for preparation of SNCNs.

Fig. 6A shows that the PL intensity of SNCNs is almost unchanged by varying the pH values from 2 to 11, indicating that their fluorescence intensity slightly depends on the pH values. Considering the fluorescence intensity, operation complexity, metal ions properties, and practical sensing applications, ultrapure water is used for the present detection. The fluorescence stability of SNCNs is further determined by storing the sample for three months (Fig. 6B). It is found that SNCNs show the higher stability, compared with organic fluorescent dye rhodamine 6G (Fig. S4, Supporting Information). These results indicate that SNCNs are a good candidate as a fluorescent probe for metal ions detection, which may have a potential for analytical and biological applications.

To evaluate the selectivity of SNCNs as a fluorescent probe, the fluorescence intensities of SNCNs were analyzed in the presence of a series of metal ions. Fig. 7A shows that the existence of 50.0μM Hg2+ induces a significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity (quenching 76% of its original values upon exciting with UV light of 371nm). In contrast, the fluorescence intensity is almost kept unchanged in the presence of other metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, K+, Cd2+, Fe2+, 50.0μM) under the same conditions, while the fluorescence intensity is steeply decreased by further treatment with 50.0μM Hg2+ (Fig. 7B). These observations indicate that the other metal ions hardly affect the formation of Hg-S bands on the surface of SNCNs. The outstanding specificity is attributed to stronger affinity between Hg2+ and thiol groups of SNCNs, which facilitates the non-radiative recombination of the excitations via an effective electron transfer process [26]. In addition, SNCNs show a little response to Fe3+, because Fe3+ has an affinity with free hydroxyl or amino groups on the surface of SNCNs. More interesting, it is found that SNCNs do not have responses to Fe2+. Therefore, we can eliminate the interference via reducing Fe3+ to Fe2+ with ascorbic acid. Thus, SNCNs provide a desired platform for detection of Hg2+ with high selectivity.

To estimate the sensitivity of SNCNs, the fluorescence spectra were recorded after the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ (Fig. 8A). The fluorescence intensity at 442nm is significantly decreased by increasing the Hg2+ concentrations. This trend is consistent with the fluorescence quenching photographs (insets in Fig. 8A), in which the color of the solution gradually changes from bright blue to dark blue under the same conditions. These observations are ascribed to the presence of Hg2+ by presumably quenching the fluorescence of SNCNs via the formation of the stable Hg-S bands.

Fig. 8B exhibits the good linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F 0) and the concentrations of Hg2+ in the range from 1.0nM to 50.0μM, where the F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of Hg2+, respectively. The error bars represent variations among three separate measurements. The detection limit is estimated to be 0.05nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N =3), which is comparable to other previously reported values (Table S1, Supporting Information).

The excellent specificity and high sensitivity of SNCNs to Hg2+ imply that this method can be applied for detecting Hg2+ in real samples such as lake water. The corresponding results are provided (Table S2, Supporting Information). It can be seen that the recovery of five samples is satisfied, demonstrating the applicability of the as-developed method.

To have deep insight on the fluorescence quenching mechanism, time-correlated single-photon counting experiments were conducted. The lifetime of SNCNs is calculated to be 6.8±0.1ns (Fig. S5, Supporting Information), which is similar to that of the mixture containing bis(2-ethylhexyl) sulfosuccinate sodium salt, decane, glucose, and thioglycolic acid [51]. However, this value is decreased to 5.5±0.1ns in the presence of Hg2+ (50.0μM). The significantly reduced lifetime indicates a dynamics quenching occurred, further showing the ultrafast electron transfer process occurred in the Hg-S bands.

In summary, we have developed a simple, low-cost, direct, and green one-pot method for preparation of water-soluble fluorescent SNCNs by solid-phase thermal treatment of glutathione. The as-prepared SNCNs have a bright blue fluorescence located at 442nm and a relatively high quantum yield of 39.9%, which can serve as a fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ in lake water samples with high sensitivity and selectivity. The strategy relies on Hg2+-induced quenching of fluorescent SNCNs that occurs through the high-affinity thiophilic Hg2+ and thiol interactions. The present strategy may offer a new approach for developing low-cost, high sensitive and selective Hg2+ sensors in environmental applications. Most importantly, the present synthetic strategy provides a simple, green, and solvent-free route using simple chemicals as candidates for synthesis of carbon nanomaterials in bioimaging, energy conversion and storage, catalysis, and biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Nos. 21175118, 21275130, 21275131, 21305128, and 21345006), universities in Zhejiang province to the young academic leaders of academic climbing project (pd2013055) and Zhejiang province environmental protection scientific research plan project (2013A025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2014.06.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Weiping Wang is an associate professor in College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University. She received her Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University in 2007. Her research interests are capillary electrophoresis separation of new methods and the interaction between small molecules and biological macromolecules

Ya-Chun Lu received her bachelor's degree in College of Zhongyuan University of Technology in 2012. She is currently a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University and her research interest is fluorescent nanomaterials and fluorescence spectroscopy.

Hong Huang obtained his B.S. degree in chemistry from Fuyang Teachers College in 2011. Then, he moved to Zhejiang Normal University as a master student under the supervision of Professor Jiuju Feng. His current research interests focus on the development of fluorescent nanomaterials for analytical and bioanalytical applications.

Ai-Jun Wang is an associate professor in College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University. He received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University in 2007. His research interests include electrochemical sensor, functional nanomaterials and biomaterials.

Jian-Rong Chen is a professor in College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University. He received his Ph.D. degree from Nanyang Technological University in 2002. His research interests include separation and enrichment, spectral analysis and the nano in analytical chemistry.

Jiu-Ju Feng is a professor in College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University. She received her Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University in 2006. She did her three-year post-doctoral research in Technische Universität Berlin. Her research interests include Raman and fluorescence spectroscopy, electrochemical sensor and biosensors, functional nanomaterials and biomaterials, as well as fuel cells.
